questions.question:
Consider the sequential circuit shown in the figure, where both flip-flops used are positive edge-triggered D flip-flops.

[Circuit: Two D flip-flops (FF0, FF1) connected in sequence. Input 'in' and current states Q0, Q1. The next-state logic: D0 = in XOR Q0, D1 = Q0 XOR Q1. Output is Q1Q0.]

The number of states in the state transition diagram of this circuit that have a transition back to the same state on some value of "in" is ____
questions.to_find: Number of states that have a self-loop (transition back to the same state) for some value of in

understand.plain: We have two D flip-flops forming a 4-state sequential circuit. For each state (Q1Q0), we compute the next state for in=0 and in=1. We need to count how many states have at least one input value that keeps the circuit in the same state (self-loop).

keywords.explain: A flip-flop that captures the value at its D input on the rising edge of the clock, storing it as Q.
keywords.example: If D=1 at clock edge, Q becomes 1 regardless of previous state.

keywords.explain: A state has a self-loop if there exists some input value that causes the next state to equal the current state.
keywords.example: State 00 has a self-loop on in=0 if next state is also 00 when in=0.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 500 320'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='480' height='300' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>State Transition: Q1Q0 (D0=in XOR Q0, D1=Q0 XOR Q1)</text><circle cx='120' cy='130' r='40' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>00</text><circle cx='320' cy='130' r='40' class='hi'/><text x='320' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>01</text><circle cx='120' cy='250' r='40' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>10</text><circle cx='320' cy='250' r='40' class='hi'/><text x='320' y='245' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>11</text><text x='120' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>in=0->00</text><text x='320' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>in=1->00</text><text x='120' y='268' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>in=1->11</text><text x='320' y='268' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>in=0->11</text><text x='250' y='290' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>States 00 and 11 have self-loops</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: State transition diagram for 4-state circuit. States 00 and 11 each have a self-loop for one value of in. States 01 and 10 do not have self-loops.

given.aim: Count states in a 2 flip-flop sequential circuit that have at least one self-loop (next state = current state for some input).

terms.meaning: Next state of FF0 depends on input 'in' and current Q0
Next state of FF1 depends on current Q0 and Q1
D0=Q0 AND D1=Q1 simultaneously for some value of in
terms.example: State 00: need D0=0 and D1=0. D1=Q0 XOR Q1=0 XOR 0=0 (ok). D0=in XOR 0=in, so need in=0.
terms.connects: This gives state 00 a self-loop on in=0

given.plan: For each of the 4 states (Q1Q0 = 00, 01, 10, 11), compute next state for in=0 and in=1. Check if any next state equals current state.

steps.title: Build the state transition table
steps.formula_raw: D0 = in XOR Q0, D1 = Q0 XOR Q1
steps.apply:
State 00 (Q1=0,Q0=0):
  in=0: D0=0 XOR 0=0, D1=0 XOR 0=0 -> next=00 (SELF-LOOP)
  in=1: D0=1 XOR 0=1, D1=0 XOR 0=0 -> next=01

State 01 (Q1=0,Q0=1):
  in=0: D0=0 XOR 1=1, D1=1 XOR 0=1 -> next=11
  in=1: D0=1 XOR 1=0, D1=1 XOR 0=1 -> next=10

State 10 (Q1=1,Q0=0):
  in=0: D0=0 XOR 0=0, D1=0 XOR 1=1 -> next=10 ... wait, D1=Q0 XOR Q1=0 XOR 1=1, so next Q1=D1=1, Q0=D0=0 -> next=10 (SELF-LOOP)
  in=1: D0=1 XOR 0=1, D1=0 XOR 1=1 -> next=11

State 11 (Q1=1,Q0=1):
  in=0: D0=0 XOR 1=1, D1=1 XOR 1=0 -> next=01
  in=1: D0=1 XOR 1=0, D1=1 XOR 1=0 -> next=00
steps.note: Rechecking state 10: Q1=1, Q0=0. in=0: D0 = 0 XOR 0 = 0, D1 = Q0 XOR Q1 = 0 XOR 1 = 1. Next state: Q1=1, Q0=0 = 10. Yes, self-loop!

steps.title: Count states with self-loops
steps.apply:
State 00: self-loop on in=0. YES.
State 01: goes to 11 or 10. NO self-loop.
State 10: self-loop on in=0. YES.
State 11: goes to 01 or 00. NO self-loop.
Total states with self-loops = 2.
steps.note: States 00 and 10 each have exactly one self-loop (on in=0). The answer is 2.

solution.result: 2 states (00 and 10) have a transition back to themselves on some value of in.

questions.formula_note: D flip-flop next-state logic and XOR-based state transition analysis; self-loop occurs when D=Q for all flip-flops simultaneously.

questions.question:
Which of the following input sequences for a cross-coupled R-S flip-flop realized with two NAND gates may lead to an oscillation?

A. 11, 00
B. 01, 10
C. 10, 01
D. 00, 11
questions.to_find: Which input sequence (applied successively) can cause oscillation in a NAND-gate SR flip-flop

understand.plain: A NAND-based SR latch has two cross-coupled NAND gates. When both inputs are 0 (S=0, R=0), the forbidden/invalid state occurs where both outputs try to be 1. If we then apply both inputs as 1 (S=1, R=1), the circuit enters a metastable/oscillating condition because both gates race to find a stable state.

keywords.explain: A bistable circuit built from two cross-coupled NAND gates. S-bar and R-bar are the active-low inputs.
keywords.example: S=0,R=1 -> Set (Q=1); S=1,R=0 -> Reset (Q=0); S=1,R=1 -> Hold; S=0,R=0 -> Forbidden (both Q and Q-bar = 1)

keywords.explain: When the latch transitions from the forbidden state (00) to the hold state (11) and the circuit cannot settle deterministically, it may oscillate.
keywords.example: After 00->11, both gates simultaneously try to change, causing rapid switching between states.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 440 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='5' y='5' width='430' height='190' rx='8' class='bg'/><rect x='60' y='40' width='120' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>NAND</text><text x='120' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Gate 1</text><rect x='60' y='110' width='120' height='50' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='120' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>NAND</text><text x='120' y='150' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Gate 2</text><text x='20' y='65' class='sub ink'>S-bar</text><text x='20' y='135' class='sub ink'>R-bar</text><text x='280' y='65' class='sub ink'>Q</text><text x='280' y='135' class='sub ink'>Q-bar</text><line x1='55' y1='60' x2='45' y2='60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='55' y1='130' x2='45' y2='130' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='180' y1='65' x2='200' y2='65' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='180' y1='135' x2='200' y2='135' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='220' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Cross-coupled</text><text x='100' y='200' class='sub no'>00->11: may oscillate!</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two cross-coupled NAND gates forming SR latch. S-bar and R-bar inputs. Transition from 00 (forbidden) to 11 (hold) can cause oscillation.

given.aim: Identify which two-step input sequence causes oscillation in a NAND SR latch.

terms.term: S=0, R=0 (both low for NAND SR)
terms.meaning: The forbidden state where both Q and Q-bar are forced to 1 simultaneously
terms.example: NAND(0, Q-bar)=1 and NAND(0, Q)=1, so both outputs = 1 regardless of cross-coupling
terms.connects: This is the unstable precondition needed before oscillation

terms.meaning: Normal hold state, but after the forbidden state, both outputs were 1 and now the circuit must decide which to keep at 1
terms.example: After Q=Q-bar=1, applying S=R=1 means each gate sees input 1 from the other, so both want to output 0, causing conflict
terms.connects: The race condition in this transition can cause oscillation

terms.meaning: When two gates with identical delays compete to change, the outcome is indeterminate
terms.example: If propagation delay is equal, neither gate wins and the circuit may toggle continuously
terms.connects: Oscillation occurs when the circuit cannot settle into a stable 0 or 1 state

given.plan: Analyze what happens when S=R=0 (forbidden state) is applied first (making Q=Q-bar=1), then S=R=1 (hold) is applied: both gates now have all-1 inputs and the output race causes oscillation.

steps.title: Analyze each option's first input
steps.formula_raw: NAND SR: Q = NAND(S-bar, Q-bar), Q-bar = NAND(R-bar, Q)
steps.apply:
Option A: first input 11 -> this is normal hold/set-reset; no forbidden state.
Option B: first input 01 -> S-bar=0, R-bar=1 -> Set state, Q=1. Then 10: Reset, Q=0. Normal operation, no oscillation.
Option C: first input 10 -> Reset. Then 01: Set. Normal operation.
Option D: first input 00 -> S-bar=0, R-bar=0 -> FORBIDDEN state. Both Q=1 and Q-bar=1.
steps.note: Only option D starts with the forbidden state (00 for NAND SR latch means S=R=0).

steps.title: Analyze D: 00 followed by 11
steps.apply:
After applying 00: Q=1, Q-bar=1 (both outputs forced to 1).
Now apply 11 (S-bar=1, R-bar=1):
  Gate 1: inputs are S-bar=1 and Q-bar=1, so Q = NAND(1,1)=0
  Gate 2: inputs are R-bar=1 and Q=1, so Q-bar = NAND(1,1)=0
But now both are 0, so:
  Gate 1: NAND(1,0)=1, Gate 2: NAND(1,0)=1
Both go back to 1 again... this creates an oscillation cycle.
If delays are symmetric, no stable state is reached.
steps.note: This is the classic SR latch oscillation: after the forbidden 00 state, removing it by applying 11 causes both outputs to race and oscillate because neither gate can 'win' the arbitration.

solution.result: D. 00, 11 — applying the forbidden input (S=R=0) followed by the hold input (S=R=1) may lead to oscillation in a NAND-gate SR latch.

questions.formula_note: NAND SR latch forbidden state analysis: the 0,0 input forces both outputs to 1, and transitioning to 1,1 (hold) causes a race condition that may result in sustained oscillation.

questions.question:
The exponent of a floating-point number is represented in excess-N (biased) code so that:

A. The dynamic range is large.
B. The precision is high.
C. The smallest number is represented by all zeros.
D. Overflow is avoided.
questions.answer: C. The smallest number is represented by all zeros.
questions.to_find: The reason why the exponent in floating-point numbers is stored in excess (biased) notation

understand.plain: In floating-point formats (like IEEE 754), the exponent field is stored with a bias (e.g., bias=127 for single precision). This means the actual exponent = stored value - bias. The key advantage is that the smallest representable exponent (most negative) maps to the all-zeros bit pattern, which simplifies comparison and sorting of floating-point numbers.

keywords.explain: A number representation where N is added to the true exponent before storing. The stored value = true exponent + bias.
keywords.example: With bias=127, exponent -127 is stored as 0 (all zeros), exponent 0 is stored as 127.

keywords.explain: When the smallest exponent maps to the all-zero bit pattern, the zero exponent field corresponds to the smallest (most negative) exponent, making the overall floating-point number smallest when exponent field is 0.
keywords.example: In IEEE 754 single, exponent 00000000 means the smallest normal/subnormal range.

understand.visual_alt: Biased exponent mapping: stored value 0 represents the most negative exponent, stored value 255 represents the most positive exponent. This shifts the range so zero-fill gives the smallest exponent.

given.aim: Identify the primary purpose of using biased/excess-N representation for the floating-point exponent.

terms.example: True exponent = -127 stored as 0 with bias 127
terms.connects: Maps the most negative exponent to all-zeros

terms.meaning: The ratio of the largest to smallest representable number
terms.example: Using more bits for exponent increases dynamic range but biasing doesn't directly affect it
terms.connects: Not the reason for biased representation

terms.meaning: With biased exponent, the zero bit pattern represents the smallest exponent value
terms.example: IEEE 754: exponent field 00000000 -> smallest exponent; comparing two FP numbers by their integer representation works correctly
terms.connects: This is the main advantage: allows integer comparison hardware to compare FP numbers

given.plan: Evaluate each option: biasing doesn't increase dynamic range (bits determine range), doesn't affect precision (mantissa bits determine precision), and doesn't prevent overflow. The correct answer is that all-zeros exponent represents the smallest number.

steps.title: Evaluate the options
steps.apply:
A. Dynamic range: determined by the number of exponent bits, not by the bias. WRONG.
B. Precision: determined by the number of mantissa bits, not the bias. WRONG.
C. All-zeros = smallest: With bias, true_exponent = stored - bias. When stored = 0, true_exponent = -bias (most negative). So the all-zero exponent field corresponds to the smallest magnitude exponent. This is a key property allowing simple magnitude comparison. CORRECT.
D. Overflow avoidance: bias doesn't prevent overflow; overflow is a range issue. WRONG.
steps.note: An additional benefit of biased exponent is that unsigned integer comparison of floating-point bit patterns gives the correct ordering — which is exploited in hardware.

steps.title: Confirm option C
steps.apply: When the exponent is stored with bias N, the bit pattern 00...0 (all zeros) represents the exponent value -N, which is the smallest (most negative) exponent. This means the floating-point number with all-zero exponent has the smallest scale factor, representing the smallest magnitude non-zero number in normal form.
steps.note: This is precisely why IEEE 754 uses biased exponent: to make the zero bit pattern correspond to the minimum exponent.

solution.result: C. The smallest number is represented by all zeros — biased/excess notation maps the all-zero exponent field to the most negative (smallest) exponent.

questions.formula_note: Biased (excess-N) exponent: stored value = true exponent + bias; all-zeros stored value = minimum exponent; enables direct unsigned comparison of floating-point numbers.

questions.question:
Consider an excess-50 representation for floating point numbers with 6 BCD digit mantissa and 2 BCD digit exponent in normalized form.

f is a fraction represented by a 6-bit mantissa (includes sign bit) in sign magnitude form; s is a 4-bit exponent (includes sign bit) in sign magnitude form.

The maximum and minimum positive numbers in the above format are A and B respectively. Let Decimal(A) = 9.54720 x 10^(49) and Decimal(B) = 1.00000 x 10^(-50).

A. Represent A and B as floating-point numbers in the above format.
B. What is the percentage error to one position beyond the decimal point in the addition operation in (b)?
questions.answer:
A. A = 949999 99 (mantissa 949999, exponent 99 in excess-50: true exponent = 99-50 = 49); B = 100000 00 (mantissa 100000, exponent 00 in excess-50: true exponent = 00-50 = -50)
B. Percentage error = 0.5 x 10^(-5) / result x 100%
questions.to_find: Representation of maximum and minimum positive floating-point numbers in excess-50 BCD format

understand.plain: This question uses a BCD floating-point system where the exponent is stored in excess-50 format (add 50 to the true exponent before storing). The mantissa has 6 BCD digits and the exponent has 2 BCD digits. Maximum number uses largest mantissa (9.99999) and largest exponent (+49 stored as 99). Minimum positive uses smallest normalized mantissa (1.00000) and smallest exponent (-50 stored as 00).

keywords.explain: The stored exponent = true exponent + 50. So true exponent -50 is stored as 00, and true exponent +49 is stored as 99.
keywords.example: True exponent = 3 -> stored = 53. True exponent = -50 -> stored = 00.

keywords.explain: Each decimal digit is encoded in 4 bits. 6-BCD-digit mantissa stores 6 decimal digits.
The leading digit of the mantissa must be non-zero (1-9) for normalized form.
keywords.example: 0.100000 x 10^1 is normalized (leading digit is 1). 0.0XXXXX is not normalized.

understand.visual_alt: BCD floating point format: 6 BCD digit mantissa (0.d1d2d3d4d5d6) followed by 2 BCD digit biased exponent. Maximum: mantissa=999999, exponent=99 (true exp=49). Minimum positive: mantissa=100000, exponent=00 (true exp=-50).

given.aim: Find the BCD representation of the maximum and minimum positive numbers in a 6-digit mantissa, 2-digit excess-50 exponent BCD floating-point system.

terms.meaning: Stored exponent is 2 BCD digits (00 to 99), so true exponent ranges from -50 to +49
Mantissa has digits 0.d1d2d3d4d5d6 where d1 != 0 for normalized form
Format is: [6 BCD digits for mantissa][2 BCD digits for biased exponent]
terms.connects: A stored as 999999 99, B stored as 100000 00

given.plan: Maximum A: use largest mantissa (999999) and largest exponent (99 = true exp 49). Minimum B: use smallest normalized mantissa (100000) and smallest exponent (00 = true exp -50). Express each in the 8-BCD-digit format.

steps.title: Maximum positive number A
steps.apply:
Mantissa = 0.999999 -> stored BCD mantissa digits = 9 9 9 9 9 9
True exponent = 49 -> stored exponent = 49 + 50 = 99 -> BCD digits = 9 9
Stored representation of A: 999999 99
Decimal value: 0.999999 x 10^49 = 9.99999 x 10^48
steps.note: The problem states Decimal(A) = 9.54720 x 10^49 which suggests the mantissa may be .954720 -> stored as 954720 with exponent 99. The exact values depend on the specific problem statement from the original question.

steps.title: Minimum positive number B
steps.apply:
Mantissa = 0.100000 -> stored BCD mantissa digits = 1 0 0 0 0 0
True exponent = -50 -> stored exponent = -50 + 50 = 00 -> BCD digits = 0 0
Stored representation of B: 100000 00
Decimal value: 0.100000 x 10^(-50) = 1.00000 x 10^(-51)
steps.note: The minimum exponent -50 maps to stored value 00 (all zeros for the exponent field), confirming the excess-50 property.

solution.result: A (maximum): stored as 999999 99 (mantissa=999999, exponent=99 meaning true exp=49); B (minimum positive): stored as 100000 00 (mantissa=100000, exponent=00 meaning true exp=-50).

questions.formula_note: Excess/biased BCD exponent: stored_exp = true_exp + 50; normalized mantissa starts with non-zero leading digit; maximum uses all-9 mantissa with max exponent, minimum uses 0.1 mantissa with min exponent.

questions.question:
A 32-bit floating-point number is represented by a 7-bit signed exponent, and a 24-bit fractional mantissa. The base of the scale factor is 16.

The range of the exponent is ___________
questions.to_find: The range of the 7-bit signed exponent in a base-16 floating-point format

understand.plain: A 7-bit signed exponent can represent values from -64 to +63 (using two's complement or sign-magnitude for 7 bits). The sign bit uses 1 bit, leaving 6 bits for magnitude. In two's complement, 7 bits represent -64 to +63. This is the range of the exponent field, meaning the scale factor 16^e ranges from 16^(-64) to 16^(63).

keywords.explain: A 7-bit two's complement number can represent integers from -2^6 = -64 to +2^6 - 1 = +63.
Scale factor is a power of 16 rather than 2. The number = mantissa x 16^exponent.
keywords.example: IBM System/360 uses hexadecimal floating point with base 16.

understand.visual_alt: 7-bit signed exponent field: 1 sign bit + 6 magnitude bits. Two's complement range: -64 to +63. The floating-point value = 0.mantissa x 16^exponent.

given.aim: Determine the range of values representable by a 7-bit signed exponent.

terms.meaning: For n-bit two's complement: range is -2^(n-1) to +2^(n-1) - 1

terms.connects: Determines how the exponent affects the actual value magnitude

terms.meaning: 1 sign bit (for the number) + 7 exponent bits + 24 mantissa bits = 32 bits total
terms.connects: Confirms the breakdown: 7 bits for exponent

given.plan: Apply the two's complement range formula for n=7 bits: minimum = -2^6 = -64, maximum = 2^6 - 1 = +63.

steps.title: Apply two's complement range formula for 7-bit signed exponent
steps.formula_raw: Range: -2^(n-1) to +2^(n-1) - 1 for n-bit two's complement
steps.note: If sign-magnitude is used instead, range is -63 to +63. The conventional assumption for GATE 1990 is two's complement giving -64 to +63.

steps.title: State the exponent range
steps.apply:
The 7-bit signed exponent ranges from -64 to +63.
This means the scale factor 16^e ranges from 16^(-64) to 16^(63).
The total floating-point range (in magnitude) is approximately:
  Minimum positive: 16^(-64) = (2^4)^(-64) = 2^(-256) ~ 8.6 x 10^(-78)
  Maximum: (1 - 16^(-24)) x 16^63 ~ 16^63 ~ 7.2 x 10^75
steps.note: The answer expected is -64 to 63 (using two's complement interpretation of the 7-bit signed field).

solution.result: The range of the exponent is -64 to +63.

questions.formula_note: 7-bit two's complement range: -2^6 to +2^6 - 1 = -64 to +63; the floating-point value = mantissa x 16^exponent where exponent is in this range.
